user_prompt: explain the problem with I2C driver, and where it is
agent_reply: The error is an ESP-IDF 5.x driver-family conflict.
Your firmware is pulling in two incompatible I2C APIs at the same time:
ESP-IDF explicitly aborts if both are used in one firmware image. That is the line in your serial log:
E i2c: CONFLICT! driver_ng is not allowed to be used with this old driver
Where it came from in this case:
- `M5.begin()` enters M5Unified/M5GFX hardware setup, including I2C setup for RTC/power/touch/display-related devices.
- M5Unified’s I2C wrapper includes the legacy driver here:

The other side of the conflict is likely from the newer ESP32 Arduino core / framework libraries in this platform version, where some component has already initialized or linked the new IDF I2C driver. Once `M5.begin()` tries to bring up the legacy side, IDF detects both and aborts very early.
That is why the fix was to avoid `AudioManager::init()` in the SD-card smoke test. The SD test only needs:
It does **not** need M5 screen, RTC, PMIC, speaker, mic, touch, or I2C. So the test now initializes `AudioFileRecorder` directly and skips the whole `M5.begin()` path.

user_prompt: that's not acceptable at all for a audio recording device, what is it choking on? debug messages?
There were 5 calls to `i2cInit` from inside M5
agent_reply: Not debug messages. The big blockers are real synchronous work.
`startRecording took 8488 ms` is almost certainly this line in [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:268):

That is preallocating `2147483648` bytes, and SdFat’s FAT32 `preAllocate()` finds a contiguous run and writes the FAT chain. Before that, `max_prealloc_size()` calls `freeBytes()`, which calls `freeClusterCount()`, which can scan the FAT. So record start is paying for “reserve 2 GiB now.” Debug output happens after the expensive call succeeds.
`AudioManager init took 10058 ms` includes more than just audio init. In [AudioManager.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:475), it does:
And [AudioFileRecorder::init](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:222) currently calls `init_sd()`, so SD card initialization is also charged to `AudioManager::init()`.

The 5 `i2cInit` calls are from `M5.begin(cfg)`. They are not the whole 10 seconds by themselves, but they point at M5Unified doing board/display/touch/PMIC/RTC/IMU setup and probing. Also, your current config leaves some M5 defaults enabled unless overridden. Since we immediately call `M5.Speaker.end()` and `M5.Mic.end()`, internal M5 audio init is wasted for this path.
2. Biggest init offenders: `M5.begin()` hardware probing plus SD init hidden inside `AudioFileRecorder::init()`.
3. Debug messages are noise, not the cause.

For an actual recording device, I’d move SD init/preallocation off the record-button path, or drastically reduce the immediate preallocation size. 2 GiB synchronous reserve is beautifully safe for write latency, but emotionally illegal for “tap record and go.”

user_prompt: Have a look at `lib\AudioManager\AudioManager.cpp`, if we are using internal speaker and internal mic and thus having to use the push-to-talk mode, there is a path from the Bluetooth incoming audio to the internal speaker outgoing, but if we are using the mic, there's no I2S for the speaker to use, so the FIFO between the Bluetooth source and the speaker sink might fill up with samples. This is not good.
Did we already address this problem? Or should we address it now?
agent_reply: We have **not** really addressed this one yet.

What we addressed earlier was the opposite direction: `mic -> Bluetooth`. In `test_micrec`, we choke `micToBluetoothFifo()` so local memo recording can keep pumping mic samples without needing the Bluetooth consumer.
This Bluetooth speaker backlog is still present in [AudioManager.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:683):
But [pump_bt2spk](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:540) immediately returns unless mode is `Speaker`:
So in internal-mic PTT mode, incoming Bluetooth audio can keep filling `g_fifo_bt2spk`, while `g_fifo_bt2file` correctly continues collecting for the recording.
I would address it now. The clean fix is to choke only `g_fifo_bt2spk` while internal mic mode owns I2S:
- On entering internal mic mode: `g_fifo_bt2spk.clear(); g_fifo_bt2spk.setChoked(true);`
- no delayed remote audio blast after releasing PTT
- incoming Bluetooth audio can still go to `g_fifo_bt2file` for the recording
- the HFP incoming callback does not need mode-specific branching
For an external codec where speaker and mic can coexist, we probably would not choke this FIFO. This is specifically for the internal Core2 shared-I2S push-to-talk path.

user_prompt: yes use the new choke feature of the FIFO from the queuing side, if the speaker can't drain it, don't allow queuing
and yes, do add the `.clear()` call as I think this prevents short bursts of garbled sound
agent_reply: Now for the internal Core2 shared-I2S path:
- Entering speaker mode clears `g_fifo_bt2spk` and unchokes it.
- Entering mic mode clears `g_fifo_bt2spk` and chokes it.
- If mic-mode setup fails, it unchokes again before stopping I2S.
So incoming Bluetooth audio still queues to `g_fifo_bt2file` for recording, but the speaker FIFO will not accumulate audio while the speaker cannot drain it.
Verification: `pio run` passed. The first build got stuck behind a stale PlatformIO file lock from the timed-out build, but after clearing that, the build succeeded.

user_prompt: I want to develop a microphone gain manager, using `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.cpp` and `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.h`. There only needs to be one instance. The microhpone stream is assumed to be mono. The bit depth is always 16 bit PCM just like the rest of the project. The sample rate does not matter.

There should be a public facing initialization function and a "process" function that acts in-place on a buffer. There is a privately kept set of state variables. Also publically there are some getters and setters as needed.
All functionality related to tracking mic peak will now be moved to `MicGainManager`
The initialization will set an initial mic gain of 1.5x, note that a test showed that actually screaming into the mic results in about 75% full scale peak.

There is a target-gain, a current-gain, and a previous-gain. The current-gain can go up or go down once per call of process. The rate it goes up is slower than the rate it can go down (it's more important to respond to clipping).

The difference between current-gain and previous-gain is that, at the begining of "process", it uses previous-gain until a zero-cross event, then it uses current-gain and updates previous-gain. And then calculate the current-gain according to target-gain.
The mic peak is tracked using the raw unmodified values. The peak being tracked decays using the same method we use now, but called from within "process".
The target-gain is continously set to keep the mic peak at about 75% of full scale. It is clamped between 1x and 3x.
The mic peak is publicly readable so the GUI can display the peak, the mic peak after scaling with the current setting is also publicly available.
There is also to be a fixed gain mode, for when users manually control the gain. And there is to be a bypass mode.

The way the sample manipulation should work is: cast the sample to 32 bit, the 32 bit sample is multiplied by a gain, and then divided by a divisor, then written back into the original buffer as 16 bit. The gain scale is thus controlled by the divisor. We can start with a divisor of 128, and so the gain step of 1 is achievable and if my calculations are correct:

Sample rate is 16000 Hz, the I2S read buffer is set to 240 samples, so there are 66.67 calls of "process" per second. If we use 128 as the divisor, and want to double the gain automatically as slow as possible, we can go from 128 to 256 in about 2 seconds. And using 128, a power of 2, gives the compiler a chance to use bit shifting for the division. And 128 is decent resolution such that humans cannot detect any jarring transitions.
Warn me if my logic is flawed.

reason: documentation says we can save IRAM by
Enable CONFIG_FREERTOS_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH. Provided these functions are not incorrectly used from ISRs, this option is safe to enable in all configurations.
Enable CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH. Provided these functions are not incorrectly used from ISRs, this option is safe to enable in all configurations.
Enable CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_ISR_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH. This option is not safe to use if the ISR ringbuf functions are used from an IRAM interrupt context, e.g., if
agent_reply: - [Buttons.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.cpp:18): real GPIO ISR, `IRAM_ATTR gpioButtonIsr`, registered with `attachInterruptArg`.

- [Buttons.cpp](/d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/Buttons/Buttons.cpp:151): ISR path calls `GpioButton::handleInterrupt()`, which calls `Hotel::noteUserActivityFromIsr()`, `millis()`, and an optional user ISR callback.
There is also `lib/Buttons/Buttons_old_code_for_reference.ino` with old ISR examples, but that looks like reference code, not the active implementation.
I did not find project-owned usage of ESP-IDF ringbuffers: no `xRingbuffer*`, `vRingbuffer*`, or `freertos/ringbuf.h` in `src`, `lib`, or `inc`.
But ESP-IDF itself has ISR ringbuffer users:
- [i2c.c](</c:/Users/frank/.platformio/packages/framework-espidf/components/driver/i2c/i2c.c:611>) uses `xRingbufferSendFromISR` and related ISR ringbuffer calls.
- deprecated RMT and ADC DMA drivers also use ringbuffer ISR functions.
- Bluetooth SPP/L2CAP use ringbuffers, mostly non-ISR paths.

`CONFIG_FREERTOS_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH` looks risky for this project right now. The ESP-IDF I2S driver has IRAM DMA callbacks that call FreeRTOS `FromISR` queue APIs, and your code also has ISR critical sections. I would not enable this blindly.

`CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH` is probably lower risk because your code does not directly use ESP-IDF ringbuffers from normal task context. This may be okay, especially if SPP/L2CAP are disabled.

`CONFIG_RINGBUF_PLACE_ISR_FUNCTIONS_INTO_FLASH` is the one I would treat as unsafe until proven otherwise. Even though your code does not call ISR ringbuffer APIs, ESP-IDF I2C/RMT/ADC code does. Since this project uses I2C through M5/Arduino/device drivers, I would assume this needs caution.

agent_reply: Fixed. This was not a decryption-duration bug. Playback was estimating correctly from the actual `.rec` file size.

The file was short because encrypted/security builds were skipping the silence-fill logic for the missing channel. In memo mode, `mic2file` filled but `bt2file` stayed empty, so the recorder would not write normal paired stereo frames during the recording. On stop, it force-drained only the last FIFO window, which is exactly why you got `32768` plaintext data bytes: `8192` stereo frames worth, or `512 ms`.

I removed that security-level guard in [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:89), so encrypted recordings now keep the missing channel padded with silence just like WAV recordings.
Both passed. Existing short `.rec` files will still only contain the audio that was actually written, but new recordings should have sane durations.

user_prompt: is `esp_hf_client_outgoing_data_ready` something that should be called repeatedly?
agent_reply: Yes, but “repeatedly” with an important caveat.

`esp_hf_client_outgoing_data_ready()` is a readiness notification to the ESP-IDF HFP client stack: “I have outgoing SCO audio available; please call my outgoing data callback.” In this project that callback path is:
So it should be called again over time as new mic audio becomes available, especially after the stack has drained previous data.

But it should not be spammed unconditionally every pump cycle while the FIFO is already above the threshold. The current `AudioManager.cpp` logic looks like it can do exactly that: as long as `g_fifo_mic2bt.availableToRead() >= kHfpOutgoingNotifyMinSamples`, `pump_mic2bt()` can repeatedly call `BtManager::notifyOutgoingAudioReady()`.
I’d treat it as an edge/latch notification:
- call it when outgoing audio becomes available
- don’t call it again while a notification is already “in flight”
- clear/re-arm that latch when `hfp_outgoing_audio()` is called, when FIFO drops below threshold, or when HFP audio disconnects
So: yes, repeated calls are normal across the lifetime of a call, but repeated calls just because the FIFO remains ready are probably noise and worth tightening.

user_prompt: we have protections external to AudioFifo preventing underflow from ever happening, where are these for the mic paths? and how do I know if the mic is keeping up? I bet it has to do with the AGC and HPF
agent_reply: The mic protections are mostly in `AudioManager`, with a second layer in the recorder.

- [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:941) only calls `BtManager::notifyOutgoingAudioReady()` when `g_fifo_mic2bt.availableToRead()` is at least `kHfpOutgoingNotifyMinSamples`, currently `120`.

- [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:525) does still count `outgoingUnderflows` if Bluetooth asks for more than the FIFO can provide, but the notification gate is the external protection that should make that rare.
- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:1674) checks both mic and host FIFO availability before dequeueing.
- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:1684) returns early if either side has no readable samples.

- [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:1689) uses the smaller of mic/host available counts, so normal recording should not request more mic samples than exist.
- On forced drain/stop it can pad with zeros, but that is intentional shutdown behavior.
There is also mic/file sync cushioning:
- [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:917) injects/backs fills silence into the lagging file FIFO path.
- [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1002) lets one side lag by up to `1024` samples, then catches up toward `512`.
How to know if the mic is keeping up: enable/read the HFP audio diagnostics. The best signals are:
- `micI2sReadSamples` delta should be about `16000 samples/sec` while mic mode is active.
- `micQueuedBtSamples` and/or `micQueuedFileSamples` should also track near `16000/sec`, depending on which consumers are active.
- `micBtFifoFullSamples` or `micSkipFifoFull` increasing means the mic is producing but the consumer is not draining.
- `micNotifyBelowMin` increasing a lot means Bluetooth readiness is often waiting for mic FIFO cushion.
- `outgoingUnderflows` increasing means the protection failed or the BT side consumed faster than the mic path could refill.

Your AGC/HPF suspicion is plausible, but not because AGC/HPF directly cause FIFO underflow. They sit in [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:464) between the I2S read and FIFO queue. If `MicGainManager::process()` gets expensive, the symptom would be that the pump can’t read/process/queue `16k samples/sec` reliably. So I’d watch `micI2sReadSamples/sec`, `micQueued*Samples/sec`, and `outgoingUnderflows` while toggling/bypassing AGC/HPF. If those rates recover when bypassed, that’s the smoking gun.

user_prompt: when I get data from the mic I2S to enqueue it, is that from an interrupt, callback, or from a pump call?
agent_reply: It is from a pump call, not directly from the I2S interrupt/callback.
The mic I2S callback is only used as a readiness/credit signal. The actual data read happens in `AudioManager::pump_mic2bt()`:
`I2S DMA fills buffer` -> `pump_mic2bt()` polls/reads with timeout 0 -> `MicGainManager::process()` -> enqueue to FIFOs.

`pump_mic2bt()` is called from a few places: the main audio pump task, and also around `hfp_outgoing_audio()` before/after Bluetooth dequeues mic data. The ISR/callback does not enqueue mic samples itself.

user_prompt: how is `HFP_AUDIO_DIAG` being enabled for being compiled in?
agent_reply: `HFP_AUDIO_DIAG` is compiled in only when `ENABLE_HFP_AUDIO_DIAGNOSTICS` is defined.
So if `ENABLE_HFP_AUDIO_DIAGNOSTICS` exists in the build flags before `AudioManager.cpp` compiles, the diagnostic counters are live. If not, every `HFP_AUDIO_DIAG(...)` call becomes a no-op.
Where it likely gets enabled: PlatformIO build flags. Search result says it appears in the code, but I’d check `platformio.ini` / extra scripts for something like:

user_prompt: the diagnostic mechanism is telling me that during a bluetooth call session with recording active, if my local mic is off, then the redraw rate is 60 FPS as expected as it is capped at 60, but when I use push-to-talk, the frame rate drops to 45 FPS
the diagnostics show that 16000 samples goes both ways in both circumstances, and I don't detect any FIFO overflows, and everything does seem to function correctly

but the frame rate dropping is weird, the audio processing, the AGC and HPF, those are all core0, while the GUI draws are core1. The file writing and encoding are done after 2 mono streams are mixed into stereo so it shouldn't matter if the mic is on or off
agent_reply: You’re probably missing that the recorder/encoder is on core1 with the GUI, not core0.

In [main.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:258), `gui->poll()` runs on core1, then [AudioFileRecorder::pump()](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:262) runs immediately after it on the same core. The recording view sets audio mode to a 60 Hz GUI poll cap in [RecordingView.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:163), and FPS is counted only when `gui->poll()` actually draws in [FlyGui.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:163). So if the recorder pump gets heavier, the GUI simply misses 60 Hz slots.

The key detail: MP3 compression is enabled in [conf.h](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/inc/conf.h:21). With mic off, the mic channel going to the recording is synthesized silence via `queue_silence_to_match()` in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:913). With push-to-talk, the mic channel is real signal and gets mixed into stereo, then encoded by `write_mp3_samples()` in [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:1771). Same 16k samples/sec, different encoder cost. Silence is cheap; live mic noise/speech is not.

There’s also a mode-routing change: PTT enters mic mode and chokes the speaker FIFO in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:856), so the recorder is pairing real mic FIFO data with BT host FIFO data rather than pairing BT host data with generated silence. That can change burstiness and mutex/FIFO pressure even when average sample rates look perfect.

I’d verify by timing `AudioFileRecorder::pump()` total duration and separately timing `write_mp3_samples()`. Your existing long-write stats only catch SD/encrypted write duration, not encoder time. Quick A/B: temporarily disable `BUILD_USE_MP3_COMPRESSION`; if PTT goes back to 60 FPS, that’s the smoking crater.

user_prompt: For the M5Stack Core2 as we have, what are all the I2C addresses being used?
agent_reply: Using 7-bit I2C addresses, the Core2 internal bus is SDA `21` / SCL `22` per [inc/pins.h](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/inc/pins.h:16>).

| `0x34` | AXP192 PMIC | Used by M5Unified and directly by our speaker GPIO2 fallback in [AudioManager.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:1018>). Core2 v1.1 AXP2101 is also `0x34`. |
| `0x38` | FT5x06 / FT6336 touch | M5GFX Core2 touch config uses this. |
| `0x51` | BM8563 / PCF8563 RTC | M5Unified RTC default. |

| `0x68` | MPU6886 IMU | Conditional: our code notes the IMU is on a daughterboard and may disappear with another codec board; it probes internal then external I2C in [ImuOrientation.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/ImuOrientation/ImuOrientation.cpp:1>). |
Also on our schematic, but not currently implemented in firmware:

| `0x0A` | SGTL5000XNLA3 external audio codec | Schematic connects `CTRL_DATA`/`CTRL_CLK` to the I2C control bus in [hardware/thefly.sch](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/hardware/thefly.sch:2571>). The address comes from the NXP SGTL5000 datasheet: the 20-QFN part uses `0001010(R/W)`, i.e. 7-bit `0x0A`. Our `AudioManager` still says external I2S codec setup is not implemented. |

So if you run an I2C scanner on this exact setup, I’d expect `0x34`, `0x38`, `0x51`, `0x68`, and possibly `0x0A` if the SGTL5000 board is installed and powered. Source for SGTL5000 address: [NXP SGTL5000 datasheet](https://cache.nxp.com/docs/en/data-sheet/SGTL5000.pdf).

agent_reply: Your test means: the NS4168 is probably happy receiving **stereo-framed I2S**, at least when both slots contain the same mono audio. `kSGTL5000I2sMclk` is GPIO14, but on the classic ESP32 the IDF I2S driver outputs MCLK through the `esp_clock_output` path, and that path only accepts CLKOUT-capable pins: GPIO0, GPIO1, or GPIO3. GPIO14 is a valid GPIO, but not a valid ESP32 clock-output GPIO for I2S MCLK. So this log is literal:
i2s_channel_disable: the channel has not been enabled yet
are cleanup noise. `configure_i2s_shared()` allocated TX/RX channels, TX init failed before enable, then `stop_i2s()` tried to disable channels that were never enabled.

Setting `mclk` to unused would make I2S init pass, but the SGTL5000 would not have the 8.192 MHz SYS_MCLK it needs.

commit_message: tweaks of how mic input works to manage silence and switching noise
mic tweaks, db scale peak display

user_prompt: I have 5 of these PCBs made with the external codec, they simply plug into the bottom of the M5Stack Core2 running our code. These PCBs all fully work, but there's a noise in the microphone that is amplified by a MAX4466 routed to the line-in-right of the SGTL5000. So these 5 different PCBs each have a different small modification, such as an additional capacitor soldered to a different location, and I want to evaluate how effective each modification is at removing the unwanted noise.
I've been trying to do this by simply recording the WAV files but this is extremely slow. So I want a on-device visualization tool.
The function `test_noisespectrumanalyzer` is a core-1 function. It should spawn a core-0 thread. Following our convention, core-1 is for GUI tasks and core-0 is for audio tasks.
During initialization, initialize `ExtCodec` and `AudioManager` and the LCD screen, we do not really need the whole GUI, just the screen. We will want to use a full screen canvas (M5Canvas).
The audio input should be set to use the line-in-right microphone no matter what.

The core-0 thread is simply pumping in audio from I2S (reading I2S while enqueuing FIFO). Core-1 thread is doing analysis (dequeuing from FIFO), one analysis for one full screen update, but also rate capped at 30FPS.

The FFT for the spectrum analysis is basically... use a 512 point FFT, bin spacing 31.25 Hz, the bottom of the screen is 320 pixels, but use 256 pixels of it, from the left edge, for 256 frequency bins. For vertical scaling, there's 240 pixels available, we will use two different scales
First scale: pick a fixed scaling that makes sense assuming 10% of full scale is the maximum a raw sample could be. We might adjust this.
Second scale: basically a full scale that can fit the maximum magnitude expected.

Use vertical lines for the bars (use white for the small scale, use green for the full scale). If the user touches the screen, then the touch coordinate selects a particular frequency bin, if one is selected, draw it with orange instead.
If the user touches off of the graph area, then deselect the frequency. If the user pressed BtnA or BtnC, then select the left or right adjacent frequency bin relative to the currently selected one.

So this there's about 64 pixels blank on the right side of the screen. Use the smallest font, display the RMS power, and if selected, display the frequency selected and the magnitude at that frequency.
